```{r ensemble, fig.width=10, fig.height=5}
  factor(ifelse(late_votes >= 2, "Late", "On_Time"),   # tie -> Late
         levels = c("On_Time", "Late"))
}

# Evaluate ensemble across all CV folds
acc_ensemble <- numeric(k)

for (i in seq_len(k)) {
  fold_preds <- all_preds[[i]]
  pred_cols  <- fold_preds[, c("c50_base", "c50_boost", "c50_cost", "rf")]
  ensemble_pred <- hard_vote(pred_cols)
  acc_ensemble[i] <- mean(ensemble_pred == fold_preds$actual)
}

# Compare all models + ensemble
acc_all <- cbind(acc_mat, Ensemble = acc_ensemble)
boxplot(acc_all,
        main = paste0(k, "-Fold CV - Accuracy: Models vs. Ensemble"),
        ylab = "Accuracy",
        col = c("grey80", "steelblue", "firebrick",
                "forestgreen", "gold"),
        las = 2, cex.axis = 0.8)

cat("-- Ensemble mean accuracy --\n")
cat(round(mean(acc_ensemble), 4), "\n")
cat("-- Ensemble SD --\n")
cat(round(sd(acc_ensemble), 4), "\n")
```

Ensembling does not guarantee higher accuracy; its value lies in
*stability*. If the ensemble exhibits lower variability across folds
than individual models, it provides more predictable behaviour, which
may be operationally preferable even if peak accuracy is slightly lower.
Ensembling redistributes error rather than eliminating it, and whether
this redistribution is desirable depends on Olist's tolerance for
different types of mistakes.

## Ablation Test: Does Seller Segmentation Add Value?

The two-stage design rests on the claim that seller-level segmentation
provides predictive signal beyond what is captured by order-level
features alone. This claim is tested directly by re-running the Random
Forest (the strongest model) with and without the `cluster_label`
feature under the same cross-validation folds.

```{r ablation-test}
# Prepare data without cluster_label
model_data_no_cluster <- model_data[, !(names(model_data) %in% "cluster_label")]

# We already calculated these in the previous cross-validation chunk.
acc_with <- acc_mat[, "Random_Forest"]
auc_with <- auc_mat[, "Random_Forest"]

# Prepare storage for the "Without Cluster" runs
acc_without <- numeric(k)
auc_without <- numeric(k)

for (i in seq_len(k)) {
  test_idx   <- folds[[i]]

  # WITHOUT cluster_label
  train_wo <- ROSE::ROSE(is_late ~ .,
                          data = model_data_no_cluster[-test_idx, ],
                          seed = 42)$data
                          

  rf_wo <- ranger::ranger(is_late ~ ., data = train_wo,
                             num.trees = 500, probability = TRUE)
                             
  pwo_prob <- predict(rf_wo, model_data_no_cluster[test_idx, ])$predictions[, "Late"]
  pwo <- factor(ifelse(pwo_prob > 0.5, "Late", "On_Time"),
           levels = c("On_Time", "Late"))
  
  acc_without[i] <- mean(pwo == model_data_no_cluster[test_idx, "is_late"])
  auc_without[i] <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(
    model_data_no_cluster[test_idx, "is_late"], pwo_prob,
    levels = c("On_Time", "Late"), direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)))
}

# Compare
ablation <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Mean Accuracy", "SD Accuracy", "Mean AUC", "SD AUC"),
  With_Cluster    = c(round(mean(acc_with), 4), round(sd(acc_with), 4),
                      round(mean(auc_with), 4), round(sd(auc_with), 4)),
  Without_Cluster = c(round(mean(acc_without), 4), round(sd(acc_without), 4),
                      round(mean(auc_without), 4), round(sd(auc_without), 4))
)
print(ablation)

# Visual comparison
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
boxplot(cbind(With = acc_with, Without = acc_without),
        main = "Ablation: Accuracy", ylab = "Accuracy",
        col = c("forestgreen", "grey70"))
boxplot(cbind(With = auc_with, Without = auc_without),
        main = "Ablation: AUC", ylab = "AUC",
        col = c("forestgreen", "grey70"))
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

If the model with `cluster_label` consistently outperforms the model
without it across all five folds, this validates the two-stage design,
indicating that the unsupervised seller segmentation captures
information about delivery risk that is not redundant with order-level
features alone. If performance is similar, the segmentation may still
retain strategic value for seller management, even if it does not
improve tactical prediction.

## Model Comparison Summary

```{r model-summary}
# Compile results from the single train-test split
cat("==================================================\n")
cat("         MODEL COMPARISON (Test Set)              \n")
cat("==================================================\n\n")

models <- list(
  "C5.0 Baseline" = list(preds = dt_base_preds, probs = dt_base_probs),
  "C5.0 Boosted"  = list(preds = dt_boost_preds, probs = dt_boost_probs),
  "C5.0 Cost"     = list(preds = dt_cost_preds, probs = dt_cost_probs),
  "Random Forest"  = list(preds = rf_preds, probs = rf_probs)
)

for (name in names(models)) {
  preds <- models[[name]]$preds
  probs <- models[[name]]$probs

  cm <- caret::confusionMatrix(preds, test_data$is_late, positive = "Late")

  # AUC only if probabilities are available (not for cost-sensitive C5.0)
  if (all(is.na(probs))) {
    auc_val <- NA
  } else {
    auc_val <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(
      test_data$is_late, probs,
      levels = c("On_Time", "Late"), direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)))
  }

  cat("-- ", name, " --\n")
  cat("  Accuracy:    ", round(cm$overall["Accuracy"], 4), "\n")
  cat("  Sensitivity: ", round(cm$byClass["Sensitivity"], 4),
      "  (recall for Late)\n")
  cat("  Specificity: ", round(cm$byClass["Specificity"], 4), "\n")
  cat("  Precision:   ", round(cm$byClass["Pos Pred Value"], 4), "\n")
  cat("  F1:          ", round(cm$byClass["F1"], 4), "\n")
  if (is.na(auc_val)) {
    cat("  AUC:          N/A (cost matrix - no probabilities)\n\n")
  } else {
    cat("  AUC:         ", round(auc_val, 4), "\n\n")
  }
}
```

## Model Reflection

The results reveal patterns that require careful interpretation rather
than a simple declaration of a "winning" model. The relevant question is
not *which model is right?* but *which risks are acceptable, and how
should disagreement be managed under uncertainty?*

**Accuracy alone is insufficient for imbalanced problems.** The Random
Forest achieves the highest accuracy (77.8%), but this metric
systematically rewards conservative behaviour. A model that rarely
predicts Late can achieve high accuracy by correctly classifying the
dominant On_Time class while providing limited operational value. A high
accuracy can coexist with operational blindness, particularly when the
event of interest is rare.

**The precision-recall trade-off reflects a decision problem, not a
modelling problem.** The four models span a clear spectrum: the
cost-sensitive C5.0 achieves the highest recall (73.1%), capturing
nearly three-quarters of truly late orders, but at a precision of only
8.6%, meaning most "Late" predictions are false positives. In contrast,
the Random Forest exhibits higher precision (13.7%) but lower recall
(44.6%), missing more than half of late deliveries. As highlighted in
Lecture 5, the appropriate position on this spectrum depends on the
relative costs of acting too frequently versus acting too late.

**Different stakeholders require different operating points.** This is
where the two-audience framing becomes decisive:

For **Olist's customer service team** (tactical, order-level), recall
should be prioritised. A false positive proactively contacting a
customer whose order will arrive on time, incurs at most a minor cost
and may even create a positive experience, while a false negative,
failing to identify a late delivery, leads to customer dissatisfaction,
low review scores, and reactive support costs. The *cost-sensitive C5.0*
model, with a recall of 73.1%, is therefore the recommended model for
this use case.

For **Olist's account management team** (strategic, seller-level),
precision is more important. Reaching out to a seller about performance
issues is a resource-intensive intervention that consumes account
manager time and may harm the relationship if the signal is incorrect.
In this context, the *Random Forest* or *boosted C5.0* models-being more
selective in their predictions-are better suited.

**Stability matters as much as performance level.** The cross-validation
results show that the Random Forest is the most stable model across
folds (accuracy SD = 0.005, AUC SD = 0.005), while the cost-sensitive
C5.0 exhibits the highest variability (accuracy SD = 0.017). For a
production system requiring predictable behaviour, the Random Forest
provides the most reliable baseline, whereas the cost-sensitive C5.0
offers higher recall at the cost of reduced stability across different
data samples.

**Ensembling redistributes error rather than eliminating it.** The
hard-voting ensemble moderates individual model predictions; ensembling
should be understood as a mechanism for managing how uncertainty is
expressed in decisions rather than as a guaranteed performance
improvement. It may reduce extreme failures, but does so by trading peak
performance for increased stability.

**The seller segment adds demonstrable value.** The ablation test
provides strong evidence for the two-stage design: including
`cluster_label` increases the Random Forest's mean AUC from 0.668 to
0.692 across all five folds, while also *reducing* variance (SD 0.005 vs
0.007). This indicates that seller segmentation captures delivery risk
information that is not redundant with order-level features alone. The
improvement is consistent rather than driven by a single split, thereby
validating the core analytical claim of the project.

# 7. Conclusion and Recommendations

## Summary of Findings

This project aims to shift Olist from reactive to proactive delivery
management through a two-stage analytical pipeline, and the results
validate this approach on both fronts.

**Stage 1 (Seller Segmentation)** identified four distinct seller
performance profiles using K-Means clustering on historical logistics
data. The segmentation effectively separates delivery risk, with the
High_Risk segment exhibiting a 16.2% late delivery rate-more than three
times the 5.2% observed among High_Reliability sellers. This separation
is not an artefact of the clustering algorithm but reflects genuine
differences in seller dispatch speed, freight cost structure, and
operational capacity.

**Stage 2 (Order-Level Classification)** demonstrated that late
deliveries can be predicted at the time of order placement using a
combination of seller segment membership, order characteristics, and
geographic features. The seller segment label (`cluster_label`) emerged
as the most important predictor, appearing in 100% of splits in the C5.0
baseline model. The ablation test confirmed this quantitatively, as
removing the seller segment reduced the Random Forest's mean AUC from
0.692 to 0.668 across all five cross-validation folds. The two-stage
design is therefore not merely a structural choice but contributes
measurable predictive value.

## Recommendations

The analytical pipeline yields recommendations at two levels, aligned
with the two-stage design and the two target audiences.

**For Olist's Account Management Team (Strategic - Seller-Level)**

The seller segmentation identifies a clearly separable High_Risk cluster
with a late delivery rate of 16.2%, more than three times the platform
average. Account managers should prioritise this segment for targeted
interventions, including structured performance reviews, logistics
coaching, and, where chronic underperformance persists, graduated
penalty structures such as reduced visibility in search rankings or
mandatory shipping insurance. The Premium_Freight cluster, despite low
late rates, exhibits elevated average freight costs; these sellers may
benefit from carrier renegotiation support or fulfilment hub
partnerships rather than performance penalties. Account managers should
also monitor Low_Volume sellers as they scale, since their segment
metrics are inherently less stable and may shift as order volume grows.

**For Olist's Customer Service Operations (Tactical - Order-Level)**

The classification models demonstrate that late delivery risk can be
estimated at the time of order placement with meaningful discriminative
ability (best AUC: 0.692). Customer service should adopt the
cost-sensitive C5.0 model for its operational alert pipeline, accepting
the higher false positive rate (specificity: 47.3%) in exchange for
capturing 71.9% of truly late orders. In practice, this means that for
flagged orders, customer service can send preemptive status updates,
offer delivery timeline extensions, or prepare expedited shipping
alternatives before the customer experiences frustration. For orders
that are not flagged, the standard reactive workflow remains
appropriate. The precise threshold for triggering proactive contact
should be calibrated in collaboration with Olist's operations team,
reflecting the available contact capacity and the marginal cost of each
outreach.

## Limitations

Several limitations qualify the conclusions drawn above.

First, **model performance remains modest in absolute terms**. The
best-performing model (Random Forest) achieves an AUC of 0.692 which is
substantially above random (0.5) but far from a reliable
individual-order prediction system. Precision for the Late class does
not exceed 13.4% in any model, meaning that the majority of flagged
orders will arrive on time. This limits the operational efficiency of
proactive contact and implies that the system is better suited as a
*prioritisation* tool (rank-ordering orders by risk) than as a binary
alerting system.

Second, **feature availability at prediction time is constrained**. The
model deliberately excludes post-purchase variables (e.g., carrier
handoff date, tracking events) to simulate prediction at the point of
sale. This design choice ensures operational relevance but removes
precisely the variables that carry the strongest predictive signal for
delivery outcomes. A production system with access to real-time
logistics data would likely outperform the models presented here.

Third, **the ROSE oversampling procedure introduces synthetic training
examples** that may not fully represent the structure of the minority
class. While ROSE was applied only to training data, preserving the
real-world distribution in the test set, the synthetic examples may
introduce noise that partly explains the high false positive rates
observed across all models.

Fourth, **the text analytics component operates on raw Portuguese
tokens** without lemmatisation, stemming, or translation. This limits
the granularity of the topic model and means that morphological variants
of the same concept are treated as distinct features. A more refined NLP
pipeline with Portuguese-specific preprocessing tools (e.g., the
`udpipe` package with a Portuguese model) would yield sharper topic
separation and more interpretable results.

Fifth, **temporal dynamics are not modelled**. The analysis treats the
dataset as a static cross-section, but in practice, seller performance
evolves over time. A seller classified as High_Risk in this analysis may
have improved (or deteriorated) during the observation period.
Incorporating temporal features, such as a recent trend in late rate or
time since first order, would better capture this evolution.

## Reflection on Advanced Techniques

This section addresses on how advanced techniques (deep learning, text
analytics, and reinforcement learning) could, in principle, be applied
to the problem. The focus is on what value they could add, what would be
required, and what constraints might limit their usefulness.

### Deep Learning

Deep learning, excels at learning rich representations from
high-dimensional unstructured data such as images. In this project,
however, the data is fundamentally tabular: structured features at the
order and seller level with well-defined meanings. Deep learning's
representational capacity offers limited advantage in this context for
several reasons.

First, the dataset contains approximately 110,000 observations with 11
predictors, a regime where tree-based models (C5.0, Random Forest) are
well-established as strong performers. Deep learning models typically
require substantially larger datasets to realise their representational
advantage and are prone to overfitting on smaller tabular datasets.
Second, interpretability is a core requirement of this project. The
business audiences (account managers and customer service) need to
understand *why* an order or seller is flagged, not just the prediction
itself. Decision trees and feature importance plots provide this
transparency directly; neural networks would require post-hoc
explanation methods (e.g., SHAP values) that add complexity without
clear benefit. Third, the feature space is already low-dimensional and
well-engineered, there are no raw, unprocessed inputs (such as images or
text sequences) that would benefit from the hierarchical feature
learning that deep learning provides.

One scenario where deep learning could add value is if the problem were
reformulated to incorporate unstructured data directly. For example, if
product images were available, a convolutional neural network could
learn visual features (e.g., product size, packaging quality) that
correlate with delivery complexity. Similarly, if raw review text were
modelled as a sequence (rather than as a bag of words), recurrent or
transformer-based architectures could capture more nuanced patterns.
However, both scenarios would require substantial additional data and
infrastructure that fall outside the scope of this project.

### Text Analytics

Text analytics was implemented in this project through LDA topic
modelling on Portuguese review text, comparing themes in late-delivery
versus on-time reviews. This implementation serves a complementary,
qualitative role: it validates the business case by showing that late
deliveries generate *qualitatively different* customer feedback, not
just lower scores.

The current implementation is deliberately basic. Reviews were tokenised
and filtered but not lemmatised, stemmed, or translated. More advanced
approaches could extend this analysis in several directions. Sentiment
analysis using Portuguese-specific lexicons or multilingual transformer
models (e.g., multilingual BERT) could quantify the intensity of
negative sentiment associated with late deliveries, moving beyond the
binary Late/On_Time grouping. Aspect-based sentiment analysis could
identify which specific aspects of the delivery experience (e.g.,
communication, packaging, carrier performance) drive dissatisfaction.
Additionally, review text features (e.g., sentiment scores, topic
proportions) could be integrated as predictors in the classification
models, potentially improving late-delivery prediction by capturing
customer-facing signals that structured order data does not.

However, incorporating text features into the prediction pipeline
introduces a conceptual complication: review text is generated *after*
delivery, meaning it cannot be used for prediction at the point of sale.
Text analytics therefore remains most valuable as a diagnostic and
strategic tool, helping Olist understand the *consequences* of late
delivery, rather than as a predictive input.

### Reinforcement Learning

Reinforcement learning (RL), addresses problems of sequential
decision-making under uncertainty, where an agent learns a policy by
interacting with an environment over time. The late-delivery prediction
problem, as currently framed, is a static classification task, each
order is evaluated independently at a single point in time and therefore
does not naturally lend itself to an RL formulation.

However, a meaningful RL application could emerge if the problem were
reformulated around Olist's *intervention decisions*. Consider the
following scenario: when an order is flagged as high-risk, Olist must
decide what action to take, send a preemptive notification, contact the
seller, offer expedited shipping, or do nothing. Each action has a cost,
and its effectiveness depends on the specific circumstances (seller
profile, product type, geographic distance). The outcome of the action
(whether the customer leaves a negative review, whether the delivery is
ultimately late) provides delayed feedback. This sequential decision
problem - repeatedly choosing interventions across thousands of orders
and learning which actions are most effective for which risk profiles,
maps naturally onto an RL framework.

In such a formulation, the *state* would include order features and the
seller's risk profile, the *actions* would be the available
interventions, and the *reward* would reflect a combination of customer
satisfaction (review scores), operational cost, and delivery outcome. A
Q-learning agent could learn which interventions are most cost-effective
for different types of orders, adapting its policy over time as the
platform accumulates more data. The key challenge would be defining a
realistic reward structure that balances competing objectives (e.g., the
cost of proactive contact versus the reputational cost of inaction),
that RL agents are highly sensitive to reward design choices.

This formulation would require real-time data on intervention outcomes,
which Olist does not currently make available in the public dataset and
would need careful consideration of exploration-exploitation trade-offs
in a setting where poor exploration choices (e.g., choosing not to
intervene on a truly late order) have real consequences for customers.
Nevertheless, it represents a conceptually sound extension that would
move Olist from *predicting* late deliveries to *optimally managing*
them.

## Final Remark

This project demonstrates that a two-stage analytical pipeline,
unsupervised seller segmentation followed by supervised order-level
classification, provides a coherent framework for shifting Olist's
delivery management from reactive to proactive. The seller segmentation
identifies structurally distinct performance profiles that are
operationally meaningful, and the ablation test confirms that this
segmentation adds measurable predictive value to the order-level models.
The text analytics component complements the quantitative pipeline by
revealing the qualitative consequences of late delivery in customers'
own words.

The models presented here are not production-ready, their precision is
too low for fully automated decision-making, and several data
limitations constrain their discriminative power. However, the
analytical design is sound: it separates the strategic question (*which
sellers are structurally high-risk?*) from the tactical question (*which
orders should be flagged?*), enabling different business units to act on
insights at the appropriate level. The path to deployment would involve
enriching the feature set with real-time logistics data, refining the
text analytics pipeline with Portuguese-specific NLP tools, and
calibrating decision thresholds in collaboration with Olist's operations
team.

# 8. Bibliography

Buck, A. (n.d.). Ecommerce share of retail sales [Updated 2026].
<https://www.mobiloud.com/blog/ecommerce-percentage-of-retail-sales>

Global ecommerce Sales Growth (2022-2028) [Updated Oct 2024]. (n.d.).
Oberlo.
<https://www.oberlo.com/statistics/global-ecommerce-sales-growth>
